message: Node started: Implement a solution for the Topological Map problem using the following roadmap: 1) Data Representation: First, parse the 50x50 input grid. Construct an adjacency graph G where each node represents a color (1 to M). An edge exists between color A and color B if there is at least one pair of adjacent cells (up, down, left, right) in the input grid containing colors A and B. Store this as an adjacency list or adjacency matrix. 2) Core Logical Requirements: The output grid must satisfy two invariants: (a) Connectivity: For every color i in 1..M, the set of cells containing color i must form a single connected component (using 4-connectivity). (b) Adjacency Preservation: Two colors i and j are adjacent in the output grid if and only if they were adjacent in the input graph G. 3) Optimization Goal: Maximize the number of 0 (empty) cells. This is equivalent to finding the smallest possible footprint for the color graph on the 50x50 grid. 4) High-Level Strategy: Treat this as a graph embedding problem. Instead of trying to shrink the original grid, attempt to build a new, compact skeleton. A viable approach is to find a layout for the graph G on the grid. Start by finding a spanning tree or a path that visits all nodes in G. Map these nodes to a sequence of cells in the grid such that adjacent nodes in the graph are placed in adjacent cells. To maximize 0s, use a 'snake' or 'spiral' pattern to place the colors, or use a BFS-based placement to keep the colors clustered. A robust baseline is to identify a 'backbone' of cells for each color. For each color i, assign a single cell (x, y). For every edge (i, j) in G, ensure cell(i) and cell(j) are adjacent. This is a subgraph isomorphism problem (embedding G into a grid graph). If M is small, use a greedy placement or a backtracking search to find coordinates (x_i, y_i) for each color i such that all adjacency constraints are met and no two colors occupy the same cell. Once coordinates are assigned, the remaining cells are 0. Ensure the connectivity of each color is maintained by potentially expanding a single cell into a small line if necessary, though a single cell per color is the most efficient if the graph allows it.
